title: The A.I
raw_response: {"rating": -0.75, "lean": "Center", "explanation": "This is an opinion/personal essay by a humanities professor reflecting on how A.I. has affected his teaching. The piece is largely apolitical, focused on pedagogy and the value of humanistic education. While the author implicitly values liberal arts education and community-building — themes that can loosely align with left-of-center cultural values — the piece does not engage with partisan political issues directly. The author's perspective is that of a practitioner sharing professional experience rather than advancing an ideological agenda. The rating sits near center with a very slight left lean. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author frames the humanities and liberal arts education as essential for citizenship and critical thinking, implicitly pushing back against market-driven or ROI-focused critiques of education, which loosely aligns with left-of-center cultural values favoring broad public education. (2) The author dismisses 'return-on-investment literalists who willfully misunderstand the fit between school and work' — a characterization that frames conservative/market-oriented critics of higher education negatively. (3) The author emphasizes community-building, face-to-face human connection, and collective problem-solving as core values, which carry mild left-of-center cultural connotations. (4) However, the author also emphasizes personal responsibility, doing one's own work, and skepticism of technology dependence — values that don't align neatly with either political direction. (5) The piece contains no partisan political content, no references to political figures or policies, and the core subject matter is pedagogical rather than political, keeping the rating very close to center."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion/personal essay by a humanities professor reflecting on how A.I. has affected his teaching. The piece is largely apolitical, focused on pedagogy and the value of humanistic education. While the author implicitly values liberal arts education and community-building — themes that can loosely align with left-of-center cultural values — the piece does not engage with partisan political issues directly. The author's perspective is that of a practitioner sharing professional experience rather than advancing an ideological agenda. The rating sits near center with a very slight left lean. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author frames the humanities and liberal arts education as essential for citizenship and critical thinking, implicitly pushing back against market-driven or ROI-focused critiques of education, which loosely aligns with left-of-center cultural values favoring broad public education. (2) The author dismisses 'return-on-investment literalists who willfully misunderstand the fit between school and work' — a characterization that frames conservative/market-oriented critics of higher education negatively. (3) The author emphasizes community-building, face-to-face human connection, and collective problem-solving as core values, which carry mild left-of-center cultural connotations. (4) However, the author also emphasizes personal responsibility, doing one's own work, and skepticism of technology dependence — values that don't align neatly with either political direction. (5) The piece contains no partisan political content, no references to political figures or policies, and the core subject matter is pedagogical rather than political, keeping the rating very close to center.

explanation: This is an opinion/personal essay by a humanities professor reflecting on how A.I. has affected his teaching. The piece is largely apolitical, focused on pedagogy and the value of humanistic education. While the author implicitly values liberal arts education and community-building — themes that can loosely align with left-of-center cultural values — the piece does not engage with partisan political issues directly. The author's perspective is that of a practitioner sharing professional experience rather than advancing an ideological agenda. The rating sits near center with a very slight left lean. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author frames the humanities and liberal arts education as essential for citizenship and critical thinking, implicitly pushing back against market-driven or ROI-focused critiques of education, which loosely aligns with left-of-center cultural values favoring broad public education. (2) The author dismisses 'return-on-investment literalists who willfully misunderstand the fit between school and work' — a characterization that frames conservative/market-oriented critics of higher education negatively. (3) The author emphasizes community-building, face-to-face human connection, and collective problem-solving as core values, which carry mild left-of-center cultural connotations. (4) However, the author also emphasizes personal responsibility, doing one's own work, and skepticism of technology dependence — values that don't align neatly with either political direction. (5) The piece contains no partisan political content, no references to political figures or policies, and the core subject matter is pedagogical rather than political, keeping the rating very close to center.
